topic: reflecting on where she wants to live long-term
facts_disclosed: Has been looking at PT job postings in Lisbon for the lifestyle
Lisbon speaks Portuguese, has seasons, walkable
Sometimes misses being able to walk everywhere

turns.content: hey so I've been having some thoughts lately and I kind of want to talk them through with someone who isn't going to judge me haha
No judgment here at all! I'm happy to help you think things through. What's been on your mind?

so I'm a physical therapist and I've been living in the Middle East for a while now, working at a rehab clinic, and it's been good you know? Great career opportunities, good salary, everything. But lately I've been... I don't know, browsing job postings in other cities. Just out of curiosity at first but now it's becoming more of a real thought

That's completely normal — even when things are going well, it's natural to explore what else is out there. Curiosity about other possibilities doesn't mean anything is wrong with your current situation. What cities have caught your eye, and what's drawing you to look?

Lisbon. I've been looking at PT positions in Lisbon actually. I'm Brazilian so they speak Portuguese there which is a huge thing for me — I miss speaking my language everywhere, not just at home or with my Brazilian friends. And Lisbon has this lifestyle that just seems... I don't know, more aligned with what I'm craving right now?

Lisbon is a really interesting choice, especially with the language connection. That shared Portuguese foundation — even though European Portuguese sounds quite different from Brazilian Portuguese — would remove a major barrier. What specifically about the Lisbon lifestyle appeals to you compared to where you are now?

okay so like, seasons! Lisbon has actual seasons! Not extreme ones, it's still Mediterranean climate, but there's a real autumn and a real spring. Where I am it's basically summer for 10 months and then two months of nice weather haha. I miss feeling the change of seasons, I miss wearing a jacket, I miss the rain sometimes — nossa that sounds crazy but I do!

That doesn't sound crazy at all. Seasonal changes affect mood, energy levels, and daily rhythms in ways people don't always appreciate until they live somewhere without them. Lisbon's climate is one of the mildest in Europe — warm summers but not extreme, cool but not harsh winters, and that beautiful Atlantic light. What else about the lifestyle there is pulling you?

walkability! Gente, I sometimes miss being able to walk everywhere so much. Like just... walking to the bakery, walking to the market, walking to a cafe. Lisbon is very walkable, there's trams, there's the metro. Where I live everything is so car-dependent and I honestly get tired of that. I want to just step outside my door and BE somewhere, you know?

That's a really significant quality-of-life factor. Walkability isn't just about convenience — it changes how you interact with your neighborhood, how spontaneous you can be, how much incidental movement you get in a day. For someone who works in physical therapy and values movement, living in a place that naturally encourages walking must feel more aligned with your values. Have the job postings you've seen looked promising?

some of them, yes! There are sports rehab clinics in Lisbon that look really good. The salary would be lower obviously — Portugal doesn't pay like the Gulf — but the cost of living is also lower, and honestly at this point in my life I'm starting to think maybe quality of life matters more than the paycheck? Like what good is a high salary if you spend half your time stuck in traffic, you know?

That's a really mature shift in priorities, and it's one a lot of expats go through after several years abroad. The financial trade-off is real, but you're right that compensation isn't just salary — it includes walkability, climate, cultural connection, language, work-life balance. Have you thought about what the licensing or credential recognition process looks like for PTs in Portugal? That could be a practical factor worth researching early.

I haven't looked into the credential process yet, that's a good point. I did my doctorate in Brazil so I'd need to figure out how that transfers to Portugal. I think there's some reciprocity for Brazilian qualifications in Portugal but I need to research it properly. Ai meu Deus there's so much to think about

There is a lot to consider, but taking it step by step helps. Portugal does have some bilateral recognition agreements with Brazil for professional qualifications, so that's a good starting point. I'd suggest looking into the Ordem dos Fisioterapeutas — that's Portugal's professional body for physiotherapists — and checking their requirements for foreign-trained practitioners.
Ordem dos Fisioterapeutas, okay I'm writing that down. That's helpful. What about the job market itself — do you know if sports rehab is a growing field in Portugal or is it more saturated?

Portugal's sports rehabilitation market has been growing, partly driven by football culture — the country has a strong football infrastructure and professional clubs invest heavily in sports medicine and rehab. Lisbon specifically has the advantage of being a hub for professional athletics. There's also growing demand for private sports rehab clinics serving the general active population, similar to what you'd see in the Gulf. The market may be smaller overall, but with your international experience, you'd likely stand out.

oh that's interesting about football! I treat a lot of athletes now so having that experience would definitely help. And honestly, being a Brazilian PT in Portugal who also has Gulf experience? That's a pretty unique resume haha. I didn't think about it that way

Absolutely — that combination of training backgrounds and international clinical experience is genuinely distinctive. You'd bring a perspective that most local candidates wouldn't have. And the cultural adaptability you've already demonstrated by building a career in the Middle East shows you can thrive in new environments. That matters a lot to employers.

okay now you're making me excited haha. I need to be careful because I go from 'just browsing' to 'planning my move' really fast. I think part of me is scared to actually take the leap because I've built a whole life where I am now — my patients, my colleagues, my routine. But another part of me is like... Bruna, you already uprooted your entire life once, what's one more time?

That's a powerful reframe — you've already proven you can uproot, adapt, and thrive in a completely different environment. That experience and resilience would carry over. And you don't have to decide right now. You could start with research — credential recognition, cost of living comparisons, maybe even a trip to Lisbon to visit some clinics and get a feel for the city. There's no rush to commit, but it's worth exploring seriously if it keeps pulling at you.

you know what, I think I need to go visit. Like actually go to Lisbon and walk around the neighborhoods, check out the clinics, see how it feels. Not as a tourist, but with the eyes of someone who might live there. I've only been once, years ago, and it was a quick trip

A scouting trip is a great idea. Spending a week or two in Lisbon with the mindset of a potential resident — checking out neighborhoods, visiting clinics, testing the commute, eating at the local spots, getting a feel for daily life — would give you real information instead of hypotheticals. You could even try to set up informational interviews with clinic owners while you're there.

informational interviews, that's so smart! Okay I'm going to start planning this. Even if I don't end up moving, at least I'll know what the option really looks like. And visiting Lisbon is never a bad idea haha — the pasteis de nata alone are worth the flight, ai meu Deus those pasteis de nata

Pasteis de nata from Belem are reason enough to book a ticket. Take your time with the decision, keep an open mind, and trust that you'll figure out when — or if — the time is right. You clearly have a good head on your shoulders, and either way, having real information about your options only makes you stronger.

thank you so much querida, seriously. This was exactly what I needed — just someone to help me organize all these thoughts without making me feel crazy for even considering it. I feel so much clearer now. Okay, step one: Ordem dos Fisioterapeutas. Step two: book a trip. Step three: pasteis de nata haha. Meu bem, you're the best!

That's a solid action plan — and the best kind, because it ends with pastries. You're not crazy at all for exploring this; you're being thoughtful and proactive. Good luck with the research, and I hope the Lisbon trip gives you the clarity you're looking for!
